unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9! This unit is all about drama and scripts — the special kinds of writing used for plays and films. When actors perform on a stage or in front of a camera, they follow a script. A script is very different from a story in a book. Instead of long descriptions, a script uses dialogue (the words characters say), stage directions (instructions for the actors), and sometimes camera directions (instructions for how to film a scene). In this unit, you will learn how to read, understand, and write your own play scripts.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day.
Work through the Grammar file on Compound Nouns.
Read the Story file, “Nookwatch: The Forest Mission”, and answer the reflection questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Activities.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Key Vocabulary
Before you read the play script, make sure you know these important drama and script words. You will meet them throughout this unit.
Drama and Stage Words
Film and Camera Words
Reading: “The Forest Guardians” — A Play Script
Below is a short play script. Notice how it is different from a normal story. There are no speech marks. Each character’s name is written in capitals, followed by a colon. Instructions for the actors are written in brackets — these are called stage directions. Read it carefully, then answer the questions that follow.
THE FOREST GUARDIANS
A Play in One Scene
CHARACTERS:
NARRATOR — tells the audience what is happening
AMINA — a student who cares deeply about animals
YUSUF — a student who loves exploring the forest
MAMA HALIMA — the director of the wildlife rescue centre
RANGER DANIEL — a young veterinarian
SETTING: A clearing near a bamboo forest in Uganda. There is a wooden shelter upstage right and a path leading into the trees downstage left.
NARRATOR: [Stands centre stage, holding a clipboard] It is early morning. The sun has just risen over the bamboo forest. Two students have arrived at the Nookwatch Animal Rescue Centre for their first day.
[AMINA and YUSUF enter downstage left, carrying notebooks.]
AMINA: [Looking around in wonder] This place is beautiful. I can hear the birds singing in every direction.
YUSUF: [Pointing upstage] Look at that bamboo grove! The guidebook says gibbons like to swing between the tall stems.
MAMA HALIMA: [Enters upstage right, wearing a green hat] Welcome, both of you. I am Mama Nadia, the centre director. Are you ready to work?
AMINA: Yes! We read the animal-care manual last night. We even made a checklist.
MAMA HALIMA: [Smiling] Good teamwork. Today, our oldest lemur is missing from his sleeping nook. We need your help to find him.
YUSUF: [Opening his notebook] What does he look like?
MAMA HALIMA: He is called Ibrahim. He has dark eyes and thick, brown fur — like a soft textile.
AMINA: Where was he last seen?
MAMA HALIMA: [Pointing downstage left] Near the footpath, by the bamboo grove. Ibrahim, our young vet, found unusual footprints there this morning.
YUSUF: [Kneeling and examining the ground] These prints look abnormal. His back foot seems twisted.
AMINA: [Worried] We must alert the vet straight away.
MAMA HALIMA: Mark the trail with this tape. I will radio Ibrahim.
[AMINA ties tape to a low branch. YUSUF sketches the footprints in his notebook.]
NARRATOR: [Stepping forward] The students worked quickly and carefully. By lunchtime, the search team had found Ibrahim resting in the bamboo grove, tired but safe. He had ample food around him, but his foot needed treatment.
[RANGER DANIEL enters downstage right carrying a first-aid kit.]
RANGER DANIEL: [To YUSUF] Good work spotting those prints. You may have saved Ibrahim’s life.
YUSUF: [Standing proudly] We just followed the manual and used our eyes.
AMINA: Caring for woodland creatures is everyone’s duty. If we work together, we can protect this whole forest.
NARRATOR: [Stepping forward] And so the Forest Guardians began their mission — one footprint, one creature, one act of kindness at a time.
[ALL bow. CURTAIN.]
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions about the play script you just read.
Who are the five characters in the play?
Where is the play set? Describe the setting in your own words.
Why are Samira and Ishmael at the rescue centre?
What has happened to Ibrahim the lemur?
How do the students help? Name two things they do.
What does Ranger Ibrahim say about the students’ work?
What is the main message of the play?
Script Features Questions
Now think about how the script is written, not just what happens.
How can you tell which character is speaking? What comes before each line of dialogue?
What are the words in brackets called? Give one example from the script.
Does the script use speech marks (quotation marks) around what the characters say? Why or why not?
What does the stage direction [Enters upstage right] tell us? Where on the stage is that?
How is a play script different from the way a story is written in a book?
Reading 2: The Same Scene as a Film Script
Now read the same opening scene from “The Forest Guardians”, but this time written as a film script. Notice how it uses camera directions instead of stage directions, and how the camera tells the audience what to look at.
SCENE 1 — EXT. NOOKWATCH ANIMAL RESCUE CENTRE — MORNING
WIDE-ANGLE SHOT of a bamboo forest. Morning mist drifts between the tall stems. Bird calls fill the air.
Camera PANS slowly to the right, revealing a wooden sign: NOOKWATCH ANIMAL RESCUE CENTRE.
CUT TO: A dirt path. AMINA (10) and YUSUF (10) walk toward the camera, carrying notebooks.
AMINA: This place is beautiful. I can hear the birds singing in every direction.
CLOSE-UP of YUSUF pointing at the bamboo grove in the distance.
YUSUF: Look at that bamboo grove! The guidebook says gibbons like to swing between the tall stems.
Camera ZOOMS OUT to show MAMA HALIMA (50s) walking toward them from a wooden shelter. She wears a green hat.
MAMA HALIMA: Welcome, both of you. I am Mama Nadia, the centre director. Are you ready to work?
POV SHOT from AMINA’s eyes: she looks around at the trees, the shelter, and the animal enclosures.
AMINA: Yes! We read the animal-care manual last night. We even made a checklist.
AERIAL SHOT looking down on the three figures standing at the edge of the forest.
CUT TO: SCENE 2.
Comparing the Two Versions
Now that you have read the same scene written as both a play script and a film script, answer these questions:
What does the film script have that the play script does not? Name at least two things.
What does the play script have that the film script does not? Name at least one thing.
In the film script, how does the audience first see the setting? What camera shot is used?
What does CUT TO: tell us? Why is this useful in a film?
The film script says “CLOSE-UP of YUSUF pointing.” What does this mean the camera does?
Which version would be better for a live performance in your school hall? Why?
Which version gives you more control over what the audience sees? Explain your answer.
Comparing Play Scripts and Film Scripts
There are two main types of scripts: play scripts (for the stage) and film scripts (for movies and television). They share some features but also have important differences.
What They Share
Both use character names followed by a colon before dialogue.
Both have no speech marks around what characters say.
Both include instructions for performers (stage directions or camera directions).
How They Differ
A play script is written for actors performing live on a stage. It includes:
Stage directions in brackets, using present tense: [Walks to the door].
Stage positions like upstage (back), downstage (front), stage left, stage right, centre stage.
Sometimes a narrator who speaks directly to the audience.
Abbreviations like DL (downstage left), UC (upstage centre), C (centre).
A film script is written for actors being recorded by cameras. It includes:
Camera directions like PAN (slow horizontal sweep), ZOOM (move closer/further), and CUT (sudden scene change).
Shot types like close-up (face or detail), wide-angle (broad view), aerial shot (from above), and POV (point of view).
Scene headings that tell us where and when: INT. SCHOOL HALL – MORNING.
The word CUT TO: to show a sudden change between scenes.
Quick Comparison
Imagine a character is looking at a beautiful forest:
Play script: AMINA: [Moves downstage, gazing out] This forest is breathtaking.
Film script: WIDE-ANGLE SHOT of the bamboo forest. Camera pans slowly across the trees. AMINA: This forest is breathtaking.
Notice how the play script tells the actor where to move on stage, while the film script tells the camera operator what to film.
Grammar Focus: Compound Nouns
A compound noun is a noun made from two or more words that are put together to create a new meaning. You have already seen many compound nouns in this unit! Understanding them helps you read faster and describe things more clearly.
Three Types of Compound Nouns
Open form — two separate words: bamboo grove, stage direction, ice cream.
Closed form — joined into one word: woodland, textbook, toothbrush, footprint.
Hyphenated form — connected by a hyphen: mother-in-law, well-being, close-up.
How to Spot a Compound Noun
Ask yourself: do these two words work together to name one thing? If the answer is yes, you probably have a compound noun.
bamboo + grove = bamboo grove (a grove made of bamboo — one specific place).
wood + land = woodland (land with woods — one type of habitat).
foot + print = footprint (a print made by a foot — one mark on the ground).
text + book = textbook (a book of texts for studying — one type of book).
Compound Nouns from This Unit
Here are compound nouns you will find in the story and the play script:
woodland creature — an animal that lives in the woodland.
bamboo grove — a group of bamboo plants growing together.
footpath — a path made for walking on foot.
guidebook — a book that guides you.
clipboard — a board with a clip to hold papers.
first-aid kit — a kit for giving first aid.
textbook — a book used for studying a subject.
A Common Mistake
Some learners write compound nouns incorrectly by splitting words that should be joined, or joining words that should be separate.
Mistake: “wood land creature” or “woodlandcreature”
Fix: The correct form is woodland creature (one closed compound + one separate word).
Tip: if you are not sure, check a dictionary. Over time, you will remember the most common ones.
Memory Tip
Think of compound nouns as word friends — two words that come together to make one clear idea. Just like Samira and Ishmael worked together in the play to rescue Ibrahim, two words can work together to name something new!
Grammar Practice
Exercise 1: Match the Parts
Match a word from Column A with a word from Column B to make a compound noun. Write the compound noun.
Column A: foot, text, guide, cup, bamboo
Column B: board, book, path, forest, print
Answers: footprint, textbook, guidebook, cupboard, bamboo forest
Exercise 2: Find the Compound Nouns
Read each sentence and underline or write the compound noun.
The woodland near our school is home to many birds.
Isaac carried a clipboard to record his observations.
We walked along the footpath to reach the bamboo grove.
The ranger used a first-aid kit to treat Ibrahim’s foot.
I found the answer in my textbook.
Answers: 1. woodland  2. clipboard  3. footpath, bamboo grove  4. first-aid kit  5. textbook
Exercise 3: Create Your Own
Use each compound noun in a sentence of your own.
woodland — _______________________________________________
footprint — _______________________________________________
bamboo grove — _______________________________________________
guidebook — _______________________________________________

outcomes.learningOutcome: Recognise the layout and features of a play script, including character names, colons, dialogue, and stage directions.
Explain the difference between a play script and a film script.
Read stage directions and understand terms like upstage, downstage, centre stage, and exits/enters.
Understand and use drama vocabulary such as pan, zoom, cut, close-up, and POV (point of view).
Identify, form, and use compound nouns in sentences (e.g. woodland, textbook, bamboo grove).
Write a short play script scene with correct formatting: character names in capitals, colons before dialogue, stage directions in brackets.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Perform a short script aloud, paying attention to tone, expression, and stage directions.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the unit’s vocabulary words confidently in speech and writing.

readings.title: Lights, Camera, Action…: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 9! This unit is all about drama and scripts — the special kinds of writing used for plays and films. When actors perform on a stage or in front of a camera, they follow a script. A script is very different from a story in a book. Instead of long descriptions, a script uses dialogue (the words characters say), stage directions (instructions for the actors), and sometimes camera directions (instructions for how to film a scene). In this unit, you will learn how to read, understand, and write your own play scripts.
You will also learn about compound nouns — words made by combining two smaller words to create a new meaning, such as woodland or textbook. Compound nouns appear everywhere in English, and understanding them will help you build your vocabulary quickly.
By the end of this unit, you will read a play script excerpt called “The Forest Guardians”, explore how scripts work, practise writing your own short scene, and enjoy the full story “Nookwatch: The Forest Mission” about two students who help rescue animals at a wildlife centre in Uganda.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Recognise the layout and features of a play script, including character names, colons, dialogue, and stage directions.
Explain the difference between a play script and a film script.
Read stage directions and understand terms like upstage, downstage, centre stage, and exits/enters.
Understand and use drama vocabulary such as pan, zoom, cut, close-up, and POV (point of view).
Identify, form, and use compound nouns in sentences (e.g. woodland, textbook, bamboo grove).
Write a short play script scene with correct formatting: character names in capitals, colons before dialogue, stage directions in brackets.
Perform a short script aloud, paying attention to tone, expression, and stage directions.
Use the unit’s vocabulary words confidently in speech and writing.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day.

readings.title: Lights, Camera, Action…: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Work through the Grammar file on Compound Nouns.
Read the Story file, “Nookwatch: The Forest Mission”, and answer the reflection questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Activities.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the play script, make sure you know these important drama and script words. You will meet them throughout this unit.
Reading: “The Forest Guardians” — A Play Script
Below is a short play script. Notice how it is different from a normal story. There are no speech marks. Each character’s name is written in capitals, followed by a colon. Instructions for the actors are written in brackets — these are called stage directions. Read it carefully, then answer the questions that follow.
NARRATOR — tells the audience what is happening
AMINA — a student who cares deeply about animals
YUSUF — a student who loves exploring the forest
MAMA HALIMA — the director of the wildlife rescue centre
RANGER DANIEL — a young veterinarian
SETTING: A clearing near a bamboo forest in Uganda. There is a wooden shelter upstage right and a path leading into the trees downstage left.
NARRATOR: [Stands centre stage, holding a clipboard] It is early morning. The sun has just risen over the bamboo forest. Two students have arrived at the Nookwatch Animal Rescue Centre for their first day.
[AMINA and YUSUF enter downstage left, carrying notebooks.]
AMINA: [Looking around in wonder] This place is beautiful. I can hear the birds singing in every direction.
YUSUF: [Pointing upstage] Look at that bamboo grove! The guidebook says gibbons like to swing between the tall stems.
AMINA: Yes! We read the animal-care manual last night. We even made a checklist.
MAMA HALIMA: [Smiling] Good teamwork. Today, our oldest lemur is missing from his sleeping nook. We need your help to find him.

readings.title: Nookwatch: The Forest Mission, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In the hills outside of Fort Portal, Uganda, near the edge of a sprawling bamboo forest, stood the Nookwatch Animal Rescue Centre. It was a peaceful place where injured woodland creatures were treated and slowly returned to the wild. The rescue team called themselves the “Rangers of the Grove.”
Year 5 students from Hilltop Primary had come for a one-week project. “This is no zoo,” said the centre director, Mama Hannah, “so you must be alert. You will observe, assist, and report on animal behaviour.”
Samira looked around at the woodland paths, the high trees, and small shaded corners. “Where is the first stop?”
“In the lemur habitat,” said Mama Hannah. “One of the grooves in the tree was found empty this morning. It was the favourite sleeping nook of our oldest lemur, Ibrahim.”
The students grabbed notebooks and followed Mama Hannah into the clearing. “We will assemble clues,” she said. “Look for prints, dropped food, or signs of illness. Check the manual in the visitor cabin if you forget something.”
Musa opened the guide. “It says lemurs love to climb but do not usually travel far alone.”
“We should check behind the capsule shelter,” said Selma. “It is warm and dry. He might be hiding.”
They tiptoed toward the shelter and found a line of footprints. Musa leaned close. “That is not normal… these prints are abnormal. His back foot looks twisted.”
“We must alert the vet,” said Selma. “And mark this path with tape.”
Later that day, a young vet named Ibrahim met them near the lemur trail. “Good job spotting this,” he said. “Animals rarely show signs of injury unless it is serious.”
He paused and pointed to a small dimple in the mud. “That is a pawprint too — meaning Ibrahim had company.”

readings.title: Nookwatch: The Forest Mission, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“You mean another animal?” asked Selma.
“Possibly,” said Ibrahim. “Or a person. But no one should be near the enclosures without permission.”
That evening, the group helped clean the monkey habitat. They raked leaves and refilled feeders with edible fruit. “You should avoid feeding wild animals directly,” reminded Ibrahim. “They must not forget how to search for their own food.”
Samira gently swept the path. “These creatures have their own style. Even the way they move is full of character.”
Musa nodded. “I love the gibbons. The baby one has thick fur — like a brown textile coat!”
Mama Hannah smiled. “And it is very durable. Nature always protects its own — when it can.”
The next morning, news spread through the centre: “We found Ibrahim!” said Ibrahim. “He is safe — but limping. He was in the bamboo grove, eating slowly.”
“Yes,” Ibrahim replied. “But he will need ample rest and a new capsule shelter closer to the main path.”
While building the shelter, Musa said, “We have read about animals in textbooks, but seeing them is different.”
Samira agreed. “It is more real. More urgent. You cannot stay neutral when you have seen them up close.”
By Day 3, the class was helping with new research tasks. Selma chose to observe maternal behaviour among the red-tailed monkeys. “The mothers are gentle but watchful,” she noted. “One cradled her baby like it was wrapped in gold.”
“Describe the paternal care too,” said Mama Hannah. “See how that male is keeping watch? It is part of their nature.”
Musa and another student helped repair the fences. “These materials are economical and strong,” said the ranger. “We reuse wherever we can.”

readings.title: Nookwatch: The Forest Mission, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“Because the forest is not endless,” said Musa. “Even fertile land can lose life.”
At lunchtime, the group discussed a new case.
“There is an injured bush baby in the woods,” said Ibrahim. “It may have fallen into a hunting trap.”
“We have to stop those,” said Selma. “Why is that still allowed?”
“Some people are in denial about how bad it is,” Ibrahim replied. “They say it is tradition.”
“But traditions must change when animals are suffering,” said Musa.
“It is a constitutional debate,” said Mama Hannah. “Laws, culture, and survival are connected.”
That evening, the group prepared posters for their final presentations. One board said:
CRITICAL: Stop snares before more animals are lost.
Informal patrols help — students too!
Protect our woodland. Defend our bible of biodiversity.
Another group made a “Save the Species” mural with rhymes:
“A panda with bamboo, a kangaroo too. / Do not let our forests lose their crew!”
“A nook is home. A groove is path. / An economical fix can save from wrath. / Maternal love, paternal guard — / Every creature must be starred.”
On the final day, the students performed a short news skit:
LINA: Today, a bush baby was rescued from a trap near the woodland edge.
JABARI: Rangers report it is healing. It had a bruised paw and a cut near its tail.
LINA: In related news, locals say they are open to building better fences.
JABARI: If true, that is a durable, long-term step forward.
The crowd clapped. Mama Hannah stepped forward.
“You have worked hard. You have listened well. Now you know why care for animals is not just a science — it is a duty.”

grammar.title: What Are Compound Nouns?
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
A compound noun is a noun made from two or more words combined together to create a new meaning. Compound nouns are everywhere in English. When you understand them, you can learn vocabulary much faster because you can figure out the meaning of a new word by looking at the parts inside it.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
A compound noun is a noun made from two or more words combined together to create a new meaning. Compound nouns are everywhere in English. When you understand them, you can learn vocabulary much faster because you can figure out the meaning of a new word by looking at the parts inside it.
For example, the word woodland is made from wood + land. If you know what “wood” and “land” mean, you can work out that “woodland” means “land covered with woods (trees).”
Examples from this unit
textbook = text + book → a book that contains texts for studying.
woodland = wood + land → land covered with woods and trees.
footprint = foot + print → a print or mark made by a foot.
guidebook = guide + book → a book that guides you.
clipboard = clip + board → a board with a clip for holding papers.
bamboo grove = bamboo + grove → a small group of bamboo plants.
first-aid kit = first-aid + kit → a kit used for giving first aid.
Why this matters for reading and writing
When you read, spotting compound nouns helps you understand new words without needing a dictionary every time. When you write, using compound nouns makes your sentences clearer and more precise. Instead of writing “a creature that lives in the woods,” you can simply write “a woodland creature.”
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are compound nouns? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Three Types of Compound Nouns
grammar.explanation:
Type 1: Closed Form (one word)
The two words are joined together with no space. These are the most common type.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Type 1: Closed Form (one word)
The two words are joined together with no space. These are the most common type.
textbook (text + book)
woodland (wood + land)
footprint (foot + print)
toothbrush (tooth + brush)
clipboard (clip + board)
cupboard (cup + board)
guidebook (guide + book)
downstage (down + stage)
Type 2: Open Form (two separate words)
The two words stay separate, but together they name one thing.
bamboo forest (a forest of bamboo)
bamboo grove (a grove of bamboo)
stage direction (a direction for the stage)
ice cream (a cream made with ice)
bus stop (a stop for the bus)
wildlife reserve (a reserve for wildlife)
Type 3: Hyphenated Form (joined by a hyphen)
The two words are connected by a short line called a hyphen (-).
first-aid (aid given first)
close-up (a shot taken up close)
mother-in-law (a mother by marriage law)
well-being (being well)
How do I know which type to use?
There is no perfect rule. Over time, many open-form compound nouns become closed-form as they are used more often. The best approach is to check a dictionary when you are not sure, and to learn the most common ones by reading a lot.
Practice 1
Sort these compound nouns into the three types: closed, open, or hyphenated.
Words: toothpaste, bamboo forest, well-being, footpath, ice cream, mother-in-law, guidebook, stage direction, close-up, cupboard
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply three types of compound nouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How Compound Nouns Are Formed
grammar.explanation:
Common patterns
Compound nouns can be made from different word combinations:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Common patterns
Compound nouns can be made from different word combinations:
Noun + Noun: text + book = textbook, tooth + brush = toothbrush, foot + print = footprint.
Adjective + Noun: wild + life = wildlife, black + board = blackboard.
Verb + Noun: guide + book = guidebook, break + fast = breakfast.
Noun + Verb: sun + rise = sunrise, rain + fall = rainfall.
Preposition + Noun: down + stage = downstage, up + stage = upstage, under + growth = undergrowth.
The stress rule
In most compound nouns, we stress (say louder) the first word. This is different from an adjective + noun combination.
Compound noun: GREENhouse (a building for growing plants) — stress on GREEN.
Adjective + noun: green HOUSE (a house that is green) — stress on HOUSE.
This stress pattern helps you hear the difference between a compound noun and a simple description.
Practice 2
Create compound nouns by matching words from Column A with Column B.
Column A: tooth, rain, sun, black, wild
Column B: paste, life, board, fall, rise
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how compound nouns are formed in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Compound Nouns in Context
grammar.explanation:
Finding compound nouns in the story
The story “Nookwatch: The Forest Mission” is full of compound nouns. Here are some examples:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Finding compound nouns in the story
The story “Nookwatch: The Forest Mission” is full of compound nouns. Here are some examples:
“The woodland paths wound through the trees.” — woodland = wood + land.
“He was in the bamboo grove, eating slowly.” — bamboo grove = bamboo + grove.
“We have read about animals in textbooks.” — textbook = text + book.
“Musa opened the guidebook.” — guidebook = guide + book.
Compound nouns in the play script
In “The Forest Guardians” play script, you can also find compound nouns:
“NARRATOR: [Stands centre stage, holding a clipboard]” — clipboard = clip + board.
“The footpath by the bamboo grove” — footpath = foot + path.
“RANGER DANIEL enters carrying a first-aid kit.” — first-aid = first + aid.
Practice 3
Read each sentence. Find the compound noun and write it, then break it into its two parts.
I checked the bus timetable before we left.
The sunrise over the forest was beautiful.
We followed the footpath to the river.
My grandmother keeps her recipes in a handbook.
The blackbird sang loudly from the rooftop.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply compound nouns in context in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes with Compound Nouns
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Splitting closed compounds
Mistake: “I read my text book.”  ✗
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Splitting closed compounds
Mistake: “I read my text book.”  ✗
Fix: “I read my textbook.”  ✓
The word “textbook” is one closed compound. Writing it as two words changes the meaning.
Mistake 2: Joining open compounds
Mistake: “We walked into the bambooforest.”  ✗
Fix: “We walked into the bamboo forest.”  ✓
The words “bamboo forest” are an open compound — they stay as two separate words.
Mistake 3: Missing the hyphen
Mistake: “The close up shot was amazing.”  ✗
Fix: “The close-up shot was amazing.”  ✓
Some compound nouns need a hyphen. “Close-up” is always hyphenated.
Mistake 4: Wrong plural
For most compound nouns, add -s to the end: textbooks, footprints, clipboards.
For hyphenated compounds, add -s to the main noun: mothers-in-law (not mother-in-laws), close-ups (the main noun is close-up, so add -s at the end).
Practice 4
Correct the mistakes in these sentences:
We found many foot prints near the river.
The bamboogrove was full of tall stems.
She brought a first aid kit to the camp.
I keep my tooth brush in the bathroom cup board.
The guide book had a map of the wild life reserve.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes with compound nouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Building New Compound Nouns
grammar.explanation:
Be a word builder!
One of the best things about compound nouns is that you can sometimes figure out new ones yourself. If you know the meaning of each part, you can guess what the compound means.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Be a word builder!
One of the best things about compound nouns is that you can sometimes figure out new ones yourself. If you know the meaning of each part, you can guess what the compound means.
Try these:
rain + coat = ? (a coat that protects you from rain)
sun + flower = ? (a flower that turns toward the sun)
book + shelf = ? (a shelf for keeping books)
bed + room = ? (a room with a bed)
water + fall = ? (water that falls from a high place)
Practice 5
For each definition, think of the compound noun that matches it.
A path made for walking on foot: f____________
A book that contains texts for studying: t____________
A print made by a foot on the ground: f____________
A board with a clip for holding papers: c____________
A brush used for cleaning your teeth: t____________
A room where you sleep: b____________
A fall of water from a high place: w____________
A board that is painted black for writing on: b____________
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply building new compound nouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Perform Your Own Script
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Take the scene you wrote in the Writing Workshop and perform it. You can:
Read it aloud to a family member or friend and ask them to play the other character(s).
Record yourself playing all the characters, using a different voice for each one.
Ask your AI tutor to help you practise by reading one of the parts.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise perform your own script and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Director’s Chair Game
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
In this game, you are the director of a play. You give instructions and someone else acts them out. Take turns with a partner or use your AI tutor.
Example directions you might give:
“Enter from downstage left and walk slowly to centre stage.”
“Look around nervously, then whisper your line.”
“Pick up the clipboard and hold it high.”
“Exit upstage right, looking back over your shoulder.”
This game helps you understand how stage directions work and how a director brings a script to life.
Activity 4: Film or Stage?
Your AI tutor will read you a direction. Decide whether it belongs in a film script or a play script, and explain why.
Example: “Camera pans across the forest.” → This is a film script direction because it tells the camera what to do.
Example: “[Enters DL, carrying a basket]” → This is a play script direction because it uses stage positions and tells the actor what to do.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise director’s chair game and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Choral Reading
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
The Narrator in “The Forest Guardians” speaks directly to the audience. Practise reading the Narrator’s lines aloud with clear, confident pronunciation. If you have a partner, read the Narrator’s lines together at the same time — this is called choral reading. It helps you practise rhythm, pace, and expression.
Narrator lines to practise:
“It is early morning. The sun has just risen over the bamboo forest. Two students have arrived at the Nookwatch Animal Rescue Centre for their first day.”
“The students worked quickly and carefully. By lunchtime, the search team had found Ibrahim resting in the bamboo grove, tired but safe.”
“And so the Forest Guardians began their mission — one footprint, one creature, one act of kindness at a time.”
Tip: read each line three times. The first time, focus on pronunciation. The second time, focus on expression. The third time, try to read without looking at the text.
Activity 6: Create a Sound Effect Script
Film scripts sometimes include sound effects — noises that help the audience understand what is happening. Add sound effects to this short scene by writing them in capitals between the dialogue lines.
Example: [SFX: BIRDS CHIRPING] means the audience hears birds chirping.
YUSUF: Look at that bamboo grove!
[SFX: ________________________]
AMINA: Did you hear that? Something is moving in the bushes.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise choral reading and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Write Your Own Play Script
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write your own play script.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: Write a Short Scene
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a play script scene of 8–12 lines of dialogue (about half a page). Choose one of these ideas, or think of your own:
Two friends discover something unusual in a forest or park near their school.
A group of students prepare a presentation about protecting animals.
A family discusses plans for a trip to a wildlife reserve.
Your scene must include:
At least two characters with names in capitals.
A setting description at the top (where and when the scene takes place).
At least three stage directions in brackets.
At least two compound nouns from this unit (e.g. woodland, textbook, footprint, bamboo grove).
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write a short scene.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: Turn a Story into a Script
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Read this short paragraph, then rewrite it as a play script:
Samira looked at the injured bush baby and whispered to Musa, “We have to help it.” Musa nodded and opened the first-aid kit. “Hold the torch steady,” he said quietly. Mama Hannah watched from behind a tree and smiled. “You are learning well,” she said.
Hint: Remove the speech marks and the words like “said” and “whispered.” Put the character’s name in capitals with a colon instead. Turn descriptions of actions into stage directions in brackets.
Example Answer for Task 2
[Near the woodland edge. Night. LINA and JABARI kneel beside a small bush baby. MAMA JALIA watches from behind a tree.]
LINA: [Whispering] We have to help it.
JABARI: [Nodding, opens the first-aid kit] Hold the torch steady.
MAMA JALIA: [Smiling from behind the tree] You are learning well.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about turn a story into a script.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Write a Film Script Version
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Take the scene you wrote in Writing Task 1 and rewrite it as a film script. Replace the stage directions with camera directions. Think about:
What would the audience see first? Start with a scene heading: INT. or EXT., then the location and time of day.
How would the camera show each moment? Use terms like WIDE-ANGLE SHOT, CLOSE-UP, PAN, ZOOM, and CUT TO.
What sounds would the audience hear? You can add notes like “Sound of birds chirping” or “Thunder rumbles.”
Compare your play script version and your film script version. Which did you find easier to write? Which gives the audience more information about what to see?
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write a film script version.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Before you finish your writing, check these points:
Did I write character names in CAPITALS followed by a colon?
Did I leave out speech marks (quotation marks) from the dialogue?
Did I include stage directions or camera directions in brackets or in the correct format?
Did I use at least two compound nouns from this unit?
Did I describe the setting at the beginning?
Did I read my script aloud to check it sounds natural?
Listening & Speaking
Activity 1: Read the Script Aloud
Go back to “The Forest Guardians” play script. Choose a character and read their lines out loud. Pay attention to:
Tone: Does your character sound worried, excited, calm, or proud? Match your voice to the emotion.
Stage directions: If the direction says [Whispering], make your voice soft. If it says [Standing proudly], speak with confidence.

writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: Read the Script Aloud
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Go back to “The Forest Guardians” play script. Choose a character and read their lines out loud. Pay attention to:
Tone: Does your character sound worried, excited, calm, or proud? Match your voice to the emotion.
Stage directions: If the direction says [Whispering], make your voice soft. If it says [Standing proudly], speak with confidence.
Pace: Do not rush. Speak clearly so the audience can understand every word.
If you have a family member or study partner, take turns reading different characters. If you are alone, ask your AI tutor to read some characters while you read the others.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Perform Your Own Script
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Take the scene you wrote in the Writing Workshop and perform it. You can:
Read it aloud to a family member or friend and ask them to play the other character(s).
Record yourself playing all the characters, using a different voice for each one.
Ask your AI tutor to help you practise by reading one of the parts.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Director’s Chair Game
activities.instructionsAndItems:
In this game, you are the director of a play. You give instructions and someone else acts them out. Take turns with a partner or use your AI tutor.
Example directions you might give:
“Enter from downstage left and walk slowly to centre stage.”
“Look around nervously, then whisper your line.”
“Pick up the clipboard and hold it high.”
“Exit upstage right, looking back over your shoulder.”
This game helps you understand how stage directions work and how a director brings a script to life.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Film or Stage?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Your AI tutor will read you a direction. Decide whether it belongs in a film script or a play script, and explain why.
Example: “Camera pans across the forest.” → This is a film script direction because it tells the camera what to do.
Example: “[Enters DL, carrying a basket]” → This is a play script direction because it uses stage positions and tells the actor what to do.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Choral Reading
activities.instructionsAndItems:
The Narrator in “The Forest Guardians” speaks directly to the audience. Practise reading the Narrator’s lines aloud with clear, confident pronunciation. If you have a partner, read the Narrator’s lines together at the same time — this is called choral reading. It helps you practise rhythm, pace, and expression.
Narrator lines to practise:
“It is early morning. The sun has just risen over the bamboo forest. Two students have arrived at the Nookwatch Animal Rescue Centre for their first day.”
“The students worked quickly and carefully. By lunchtime, the search team had found Ibrahim resting in the bamboo grove, tired but safe.”
“And so the Forest Guardians began their mission — one footprint, one creature, one act of kindness at a time.”
Tip: read each line three times. The first time, focus on pronunciation. The second time, focus on expression. The third time, try to read without looking at the text.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Create a Sound Effect Script
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Film scripts sometimes include sound effects — noises that help the audience understand what is happening. Add sound effects to this short scene by writing them in capitals between the dialogue lines.
Example: [SFX: BIRDS CHIRPING] means the audience hears birds chirping.
YUSUF: Look at that bamboo grove!
[SFX: ________________________]
AMINA: Did you hear that? Something is moving in the bushes.
[SFX: ________________________]
RANGER DANIEL: Stay still. Let me take a closer look.
[SFX: ________________________]
Think about what sounds would fit each moment. What might the characters hear in a forest?
Activities & Exercises
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Lights, Camera, Action… learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: Write Your Own Play Script and a recording of Speaking 1: Perform Your Own Script. Improve both pieces using the Grade 5 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'nook' mean?
quizzes.options: A long, narrow cut or channel in a surface. | A small, cosy corner or space where you can relax or hide. | A tall plant with hard, hollow stems that grows in warm places. | Able to produce many plants or crops; good for growing things.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A small, cosy corner or space where you can relax or hide.
quizzes.explanation: The lemur slept in a little nook inside the hollow tree.

quizzes.question: What does 'woodland' mean?
quizzes.options: A book that students use to learn about a subject in school. | A long, narrow cut or channel in a surface. | Able to produce many plants or crops; good for growing things. | An area of land covered with trees and plants.
quizzes.correctAnswer: An area of land covered with trees and plants.
quizzes.explanation: We followed a path through the woodland and heard birds singing.

quizzes.question: What does 'bamboo' mean?
quizzes.options: A book that gives instructions on how to do something. | A book that students use to learn about a subject in school. | A tall plant with hard, hollow stems that grows in warm places. | Able to produce many plants or crops; good for growing things.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A tall plant with hard, hollow stems that grows in warm places.
quizzes.explanation: The panda loves to eat bamboo because it is very tasty.

quizzes.question: What does 'groove' mean?
quizzes.options: A book that gives instructions on how to do something. | A book that students use to learn about a subject in school. | A long, narrow cut or channel in a surface. | A small container that holds something inside, like medicine or equipment.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A long, narrow cut or channel in a surface.
quizzes.explanation: The old tree had deep grooves in its bark where insects lived.

quizzes.explanation: The text says: Today, our oldest lemur is missing from his sleeping nook.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you explain the difference between upstage and downstage?”
“Can you quiz me on the drama words before I start reading?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me find all the stage directions in the play?”
“I am not sure about the difference between upstage and downstage. Can you draw it for me?”
“Can you ask me more questions about the script to check my understanding?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me compare the play script and film script side by side?”
“What other camera directions are used in real films?”
“Can you explain what an aerial shot looks like?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you show me an example of a film script and a play script side by side?”
“What does CUT TO mean in a film script?”
“Can you quiz me on the differences between play and film scripts?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my compound noun sentences?”
“Can you give me five more compound nouns to practise with?”
“How do I know if a compound noun is one word or two words?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my play script and tell me if the format is correct?”
“Can you help me add better stage directions to my scene?”
“Can you give me more paragraphs to turn into play scripts?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the play script with me? I will be Samira and you be Mama Nadia.”
“Can you play the Director’s Chair game with me?”
“Can you give me five directions and I will say if they are film or stage?”
“Can you help me think of good sound effects for a forest scene?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the places and nature words?”
“Can you use the word groove in three new sentences for me?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me understand the word capsule with more examples?”
“Can you test me on the objects and materials words?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the adjective words?”
“What is the opposite of abnormal?”
“Can you help me use economical in a sentence about school?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me understand the word constitutional with an example from my country?”
“What is the difference between informal and formal?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you give me three sentences using assemble and check mine?”
“Can you help me break the word assemble into syllables?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my compound noun answers?”
“Can you give me more compound noun challenges?”
“How do I know if a compound noun is one word, two words, or hyphenated?”

A small, cosy corner or space where you can relax or hide. The lemur slept in a little nook inside the hollow tree.
An area of land covered with trees and plants. We followed a path through the woodland and heard birds singing.
A tall plant with hard, hollow stems that grows in warm places. The panda loves to eat bamboo because it is very tasty.
A long, narrow cut or channel in a surface. The old tree had deep grooves in its bark where insects lived.
Able to produce many plants or crops; good for growing things. The farmer was happy because the soil was very fertile.
A book that students use to learn about a subject in school. I opened my textbook to read about animals in science class.
A book that gives instructions on how to do something. I read the manual to learn how to set up the camera.
A small container that holds something inside, like medicine or equipment. The vet kept the medicine in a capsule for the sick lemur.
A type of cloth or fabric made by weaving threads together. The baby gibbon had thick fur like a soft textile coat.
A special book that many people read to learn about God and important stories. In class, we learned about some important stories from the Bible.
